chapters.title: Foreword
chapters.keyIdea: The women's movement changed how women see themselves, but men's objectifying habits still quietly wreck the chance for real intimacy.

content.text: The women's movement, which began in the late 1960s and continues today, has fundamentally changed how women and men relate to each other. Old habits die hard, though. Some deeply conditioned attitudes still do serious damage to both individual identity and the possibility of real intimacy between the sexes.

In this book, psychologist Gary Brooks offers a way to overcome one of the most destructive of these conditioned attitudes: men's objectification of women's bodies. He describes men's voyeuristic preference for perfect fantasy images over real women, and their habit of mentally sexualizing women even while making love to their actual partners — leaving men stranded in their own fantasies.

content.label: The Centerfold Syndrome
content.text: Brooks names this pattern the Centerfold Syndrome. Its most obvious sign is a fixation on airbrushed magazine fantasies — but the root conditioning shows up everywhere: on TV, in advertising, and in the way boys are raised to depend on women's bodies for their sense of worth, while simultaneously fearing and resenting that dependency.

The foreword, written by psychologist Lenore E. Walker, introduces several of the real men you'll meet in the book: George, who struggles with his habit of staring at other women even as he loves his wife. Arthur, who pulls away from his wife after her mastectomy because her changed body unsettles him. Chad, who cycles through one-night stands with conventionally gorgeous women but feels hollow and empty after each one. Mike, trapped in compulsive pornography use. Paul, unable to bear the thought of his ex-wife being with another man.

Ultimately, Walker says, this is a hopeful book. It argues that change is possible — that many men and women genuinely want to move toward emotional intimacy and true partnership. The men in this book, however stumbling their progress, are beginning to design new rules for how women and men can live together.

chapters.title: Preface
chapters.keyIdea: Male sexuality in America has real problems — not because men are evil, but because they've been taught a distorted way of relating to women's bodies.

content.text: Brooks opens by acknowledging that men do many things well. Most men work hard to meet expectations and care for the people they love. But in some ways, everyone gets cheated — including men themselves. This book is about one of those ways.

The Centerfold Syndrome looks critically at the sexuality of heterosexual American men. Brooks is careful to note that much of what he describes likely applies to gay and bisexual men too, but he writes most confidently from his knowledge of heterosexual men, so he focuses there.

Brooks's core thesis: most men are too voyeuristic, too objectifying of women's bodies, too competitive for sexually attractive women, too needy of validation through sex, and too fearful of real emotional intimacy. These problems, taken together, form what he calls the Centerfold Syndrome.

He's honest about what he can and can't claim. He doesn't yet have scientific proof that these patterns form a single syndrome. What he does have is twenty-three years of listening to troubled men, combined with painful reflection on his own life. The patterns he's seen are real, and they make it very hard for women and men to build emotionally intimate relationships.

chapters.title: Acknowledgments
chapters.keyIdea: This book grew from a lifetime of listening — to clients, to colleagues, to family, and to himself.

content.text: Brooks thanks his editor, Alan Rinzler, for turning a symposium presentation into a full book. He gives deep credit to the troubled men who taught him — over twenty-three years of psychotherapy work — what the stresses of manhood actually feel like from the inside.

He also acknowledges the many women who shaped his thinking — feminists, psychologists, and colleagues who offered critique and compassion in equal measure. He names specific men in the field of psychology who became mentors and friends, dispelling his earlier sense that he traveled this road alone.

He closes by thanking his mother, who gave him the drive to be his best; his father, who taught him courage through his own battles; his daughters, who brought him joy he hadn't imagined; and his wife Patti, whose partnership made the journey possible.

chapters.title: What Is the Centerfold Syndrome?
chapters.keyIdea: A pervasive pattern of five interconnected attitudes — voyeurism, objectification, validation-seeking, trophyism, and fear of intimacy — shapes how most men relate to women's bodies, often without men even realizing it.

content.text: Brooks starts with a personal confession: he grew up believing that looking at attractive women's bodies was both normal and harmless. Years of studying men's lives changed his mind. He came to see what he calls the Centerfold Syndrome — a pattern in how men relate to women's bodies — as one of the most damaging forces in contemporary relationships between women and men.

The centerfold itself — that idealized, airbrushed magazine fantasy image — has been a dominant cultural icon for over half a century. Hugh Hefner's Playboy and Bob Guccione's Penthouse built an industry around it. Today, this kind of sexualized image of women is everywhere: on magazine covers, billboards, TV commercials, video games, and beach volleyball. Most people treat it as harmless. Brooks does not.

content.label: Five Elements of the Syndrome
content.text: The Centerfold Syndrome has five main features: (1) Voyeurism — an obsessive need to look at women's bodies, which has been so exaggerated that it distorts men's ability to be present in real relationships. (2) Objectification — treating women as collections of body parts to be rated and compared rather than as full human beings. (3) The need for validation — relying on women's sexual responses to feel manly. (4) Trophyism — treating beautiful women as prizes that prove a man's worth. (5) Fear of true intimacy — an inability to be emotionally close, rooted in how boys are raised to suppress vulnerability.

Voyeurism is not simply the pleasure of looking. The problem is the scale and distortion. Throughout our culture, men are constantly bombarded with images of naked and semi-naked women. Advertising, films, sports magazines, and reality TV all teach men to be spectators of women's bodies. This has been so over-amplified that it crowds out men's capacity for real emotional and sexual connection.

Objectification follows naturally from voyeurism. When men are taught to observe, women are taught to be observed. Women's bodies get lined up, compared, and rated. Their names don't matter — they're Miss May, Miss Nevada. Men talk about being 'a leg man' or 'a breast man.' Women are reduced to their most visually striking features. And since objectified images are never enough — novelty fades fast — men are pushed toward more and more extreme or unusual images to recapture the excitement.

The need for validation works like this: most men feel deeply uncertain about their masculinity. They're programmed to crave proof that they're real men, and they often look for that proof through women. A woman's positive sexual response — her excitement, her orgasm — can feel like irrefutable evidence of his worth. Conversely, a woman's disinterest or indifference can feel devastating, like proof of inadequacy. This puts enormous and unfair psychological power in women's hands.

Despite the fact that men generally have more economic and political power than women, many men feel deeply powerless when it comes to sexuality. Women, as 'gatekeepers' to something men desperately want, hold a very specific kind of leverage that men often experience as overwhelming.

Trophyism is about competition with other men. Men judge their worth partly by comparing themselves to other men — and beautiful women become tokens in that competition. The logic is: the more desirable the woman, the greater the 'win.' This leads men to pursue women primarily as symbols of status rather than as partners. It also creates a terrifying vulnerability: trophies can be won by someone else. A woman leaving for another man is not just heartbreak — it can feel like the most humiliating possible defeat.

The fear of true intimacy is the most complex element. Boys are born needing physical closeness and emotional warmth, just like girls. But they are quickly taught that needing these things is unmanly. They're pushed to be tough, stoic, and independent. Physical closeness with their mothers — their first source of comfort — becomes something to escape, not something to seek.

When adolescence arrives, boys are hit with powerful sexual urges. But they've already learned to suppress their need for emotional intimacy. So they merge the two: they learn to pursue sex when they really want closeness. And because sex becomes the only 'safe' way to get physical comfort, it gets overloaded with emotional need. Real intimacy — being truly seen and known by another person — remains terrifying.

Why doesn't anyone complain? Partly because the syndrome is so normalized that it passes as 'just how men are.' Partly because many women have accepted the premise — working hard to meet centerfold standards and blaming themselves when they can't. And partly because the people who do complain — some feminists, some religious conservatives — are easy to dismiss as extremists.

But the syndrome harms men too, in ways men rarely recognize. It creates a painful, compulsive relationship with women's bodies that rarely delivers satisfaction. It prevents genuine intimacy. It locks men into competitions they can never finally win. And it leaves men deeply dependent on women for validation while simultaneously encouraging them to stay emotionally distant from the very people they most need.

The Centerfold Syndrome is not a biological inevitability. It's a social construction — something the culture builds into boys and men, step by step. And because it's constructed, it can be deconstructed.

Brooks then shares his own memoir. As a boy, he was drawn compulsively to looking at women's bodies — risking minor car accidents craning to watch women in his rearview mirror, inventing tricks to steal glimpses at girls. His peer group celebrated these habits; his father modeled them openly, collecting Playboy and commenting on every attractive woman who passed. Ironically, this shared obsession was one of the few ways young Gary could connect emotionally with his father.

On the corner with his adolescent friends, male bonding happened through shared commentary on women's bodies. The group exaggerated, competed, and invented sexual conquests to impress each other. Nobody actually knew much about sex or women. James Bond was a hero — the man who could seduce any woman, feel nothing emotionally, and move seamlessly on. What was rarely admitted was the desperation and frustration underneath all this posturing.

Moving into adult relationships, Brooks found himself caught between wanting genuine connection and being unable to let go of the centerfold mythology. He wanted a partner who was attractive enough to impress other men but not so attractive she'd be a threat. He stayed in relationships that were wrong because of intermittent sexual gratification. He pressured early girlfriends around their physical appearance. It took years, genuine self-examination, and a great deal of patience from his wife Patti before he made real progress.

In graduate psychology training, he expected open discussion of these issues. Instead, he found that male colleagues were even more guarded than his adolescent friends. Sexual feelings toward clients were treated as the client's fault — the 'seductive' woman — not the therapist's problem. It took years of clinical work before Brooks allowed himself to see these patterns clearly — both in his clients and in himself.

Understanding the Centerfold Syndrome starts with honest self-examination. Do you find yourself compulsively looking at women's bodies in ways that distract you from the people and tasks in front of you? Do you rely on women's sexual responses to feel adequate as a man? Does the physical appearance of a partner matter more to you than her actual personality and character? These questions are not accusations — they're the starting point for change.

chapters.title: The Men's Group
chapters.keyIdea: Eight real men — diverse in background and personality — reveal how the Centerfold Syndrome plays out in actual lives, through raw and often uncomfortable group therapy sessions.

content.text: Over twenty-five years, Brooks has watched American manhood undergo a revolution. The old roles — warrior, provider, sexual initiator, family leader — have been challenged by the women's movement, changing workplaces, and rising divorce rates. Most men are deeply confused about what's expected of them. Many end up in the offices of mental health professionals like Brooks — not because they sought help, but because someone else's changes forced a crisis into their lives.

The group Brooks focuses on met over many months in central Texas in the early 1990s. Eight men: some white-collar, some blue-collar, one musician. Three unemployed. Five white, one Mexican American, two African American. Educational backgrounds ranged from high school dropout to master's degree. None were court-ordered. None were physically abusive. None were actively using drugs or alcohol. All were volunteers, though most had come under pressure from a wife, doctor, or family member.

content.label: The Eight Men
content.text: Paul (35): rageful, possessive, unable to bear that his ex-wife is with someone else. Terry (30): charming, cynical, uses women for sex and escape. Chad (26): rock musician, loves women's bodies but feels hollow after every conquest. George (40s): married engineer, his 'harmless' girl-watching enrages his wife Sarah. Arthur (62): imposing, traditional, emotionally shut down after his wife's mastectomy. Luis (46): fiercely private, obsessively dependent on his common-law wife Mary. Fred (59): gentle professor, guarded about his own struggles. Mike (36): terrified of women, addicted to pornography, almost no real-world social contact.

The sessions begin explosively. Paul declares that women are good for 'only one thing.' Terry agrees, advising the men to manipulate women before women manipulate them. Chad says the rush of a gorgeous new body is the greatest high there is. George wants to be fair to women but can't explain why he keeps getting distracted by beautiful strangers. Arthur believes God made women and men different and that men should stay in charge. Luis says women 'can kill you' — meaning the loss of one particular woman could destroy him entirely. Fred speaks quietly of being sustained by women, not threatened. Mike admits that beautiful women make him shake with fear.

Terry's story comes out in fragments. He was charming, good-looking, and had a string of short-term relationships with attractive women. His strategy was to promise what women wanted to hear, then leave before they could hurt him. He came closest to real feeling with a woman named Cassandra — and when that ended, it reinforced every belief he already had: intimacy is a trap, women will destroy you, the only protection is to stay in control.

Chad is more self-aware. He describes the pattern honestly: he'd spot a gorgeous woman at a gig, sleep with her, feel briefly excited, and then grow bored as the novelty wore off. Sometimes, if they stayed together a few weeks, something strange would happen — he'd start to actually know her, to feel genuinely connected. And that was when he'd notice her 'flaws' and find himself pulled toward someone new. He senses he's missing something important, but doesn't yet know what.

Paul's story is the most alarming. He met Elaine when she was fifteen. She became his trophy — young, inexperienced, completely his. Over thirteen years, as Elaine grew and found her own voice, Paul became more controlling and more volatile. When she finally filed for divorce and found someone else, Paul couldn't cope. He drove by her house at night, fantasized about driving his pickup through her bedroom wall. His sense of himself as a man was entirely bound up in having sole possession of her body.

Paul's reaction — the possessive rage, the inability to accept that Elaine's body could belong to someone else — is an extreme version of a pattern that runs quietly through many men's relationships with women: treating a woman's physical being as property, rather than as belonging to a full and autonomous person.

Arthur is sixty-two and proud. He believes God made men to lead and women to support that leadership. He loves his wife Faye in his way, but his way involves keeping emotional distance and seeking validation from the young women who flirt with him at his men's club. When Faye's breast cancer led to a mastectomy, Arthur couldn't bear to be near her — couldn't look at her altered body, couldn't talk about what had happened. He describes it with painful honesty: 'I kinda hate thinking about her all cut up.'

Luis is quieter than the others. He's not particularly voyeuristic or interested in other women's bodies. But he has made Mary — his common-law wife — the single emotional anchor of his entire life. She is the only person he trusts. Her physical presence soothes him when nothing else can. When she works late, or laughs with other men, or pulls away even slightly, Luis becomes consumed by terror and jealousy. He has built everything on one woman and given nothing to anyone else.

Fred is the thoughtful one, the one who speaks carefully and supports others. He credits the women in his life as sources of sustenance. But he also hints at 'lust-driven, impulsive sexual liaisons' that have cost him dearly. He's guarded about his own story — more so than the men who are loudly misogynistic. What he does say is that he has worked hard on himself, and that the group feels like his primary social connection.

Mike is the most quietly broken. Beautiful women fill him with a kind of terrified awe. He stares compulsively, gets reported for it at work, can't control it. He has almost no experience of actual relationships with women. At home, he compensates through pornography and masturbation — sometimes three or four times a day — and feels more isolated with each passing month.

Brooks reflects: when he was a young therapist, he'd quickly redirect conversations about women's bodies toward 'clinically relevant' topics. He learned over time that this was a mistake. When men are allowed to talk freely about their feelings about women and women's bodies, something real opens up. The energy in the room changes. Men who are usually guarded begin to reveal the extent of their confusion, frustration, and pain. And through that pain, it becomes possible to begin asking different questions.

The Centerfold Syndrome isn't one type. It shows up in Paul's possessive rage, in Terry's predatory detachment, in Chad's restless novelty-seeking, in Arthur's emotional absence, in Luis's crushing dependency, in Mike's isolation and addiction, in Fred's secretive compulsions, and in George's bewilderment that harmless girl-watching could matter to his wife. Every man's version is different — but the root distortion is the same.

chapters.title: Debunking Conventional Wisdom
chapters.keyIdea: None of the popular explanations for male sexuality — not God's design, not biology, not brain wiring, not evolution, not Freud — can adequately explain the Centerfold Syndrome or prove it's inevitable.

content.text: Once we acknowledge that the Centerfold Syndrome exists and is damaging, the next question follows immediately: Can anything be done about it? Or is it just how men are built? The men in the group each had their own theory. Arthur believed God made men this way. Fred thought evolution explained it. Chad blamed hormones. George pointed to his peer group and the 'laws of the sexual marketplace.' Brooks takes each theory seriously — and then dismantles it.

content.label: Nature vs. Nurture
content.text: All explanations of male sexuality fall into two camps. 'Nature' arguments say men are born with their sexual tendencies — it's biology, brain structure, hormones, evolution, or God's design. 'Nurture' arguments say society creates sexuality through culture, learning, and social expectations. Brooks finds the nurture arguments far more convincing — not just because they offer more hope for change, but because the evidence actually supports them.

The religious argument — that God made men this way — is the oldest. Most traditional religions have portrayed women as temptresses and men as their helpless victims. Eve's original sin, after all, was seducing Adam. Brooks is respectful of faith but points out that most thoughtful religious people already interpret their texts flexibly, adjusting ancient prescriptions to modern realities. Just as few Christians today believe women must obey their husbands in all things (as Paul's letters direct), they can also reexamine inherited assumptions about male sexuality.

The biological/hormonal argument seems intuitive: boys get flooded with testosterone at puberty and suddenly become obsessed with sex. Castration reduces sex drive; testosterone replacement restores it. Clearly hormones matter. But the link between testosterone and the specific patterns of the Centerfold Syndrome is extremely weak. Women also have testosterone, and though it affects their sexual interest, they don't develop voyeurism, objectification, or trophyism at anywhere near male rates. Men's testosterone levels fluctuate constantly throughout the day and drop significantly with age — but their objectifying habits don't follow the same rhythm. The science simply doesn't hold up.

The brain-wiring argument is newer and feels more scientific. Some researchers claim to have found that male brains have larger 'visual' regions, which explains men's visual orientation toward sexuality. But geneticist Ann Fausto-Sterling, a leading expert on biological sex differences, has carefully reviewed this research and found it riddled with methodological problems. The much-cited 1982 corpus callosum study — claiming male and female brains differ structurally — has been repeated sixteen times by other researchers, none of whom have replicated the original findings. Even neuroscientists who expect to eventually find some brain differences acknowledge that the brain is plastic, changing constantly in response to experience. A brain shaped by decades of cultural conditioning toward voyeurism will look different from one that wasn't — that doesn't mean the difference came first.

The evolutionary argument — that men evolved to be voyeuristic and non-intimate because this maximized their reproductive success — is clever but unfalsifiable. Sociobiology can build a 'just-so story' to explain almost any human behavior as adaptive. But this approach has a dangerous logic: if something exists, it must be good. That leads to arguments that rape is 'adaptive' (giving inferior men access to reproduction) or that male neglect of children is 'efficient' (freeing men to impregnate more women). These aren't science — they're political assumptions dressed in scientific language.

Even if the Centerfold Syndrome had some evolutionary basis (and the evidence is thin), that wouldn't mean it's worth keeping. Human culture has changed radically in the last one hundred years — far faster than genetic change can track. Many behaviors that might have been adaptive in prehistoric conditions are maladaptive now. 'This is how men evolved' is not a reason to tolerate behavior that's actively harmful in the present.

The Freudian argument — that men's sexuality is a powerful biological drive, and that the developmental process of boys separating from their mothers creates lifelong conflict about intimacy — has more psychological substance. But even its most sympathetic modern descendants (object-relations theorists like Nancy Chodorow and Carol Gilligan) have shifted the blame from innate drives to the social structure of families: specifically, the historical absence of emotionally engaged fathers. If fathers were more present and nurturing, boys would need less 'defensive autonomy.' That points to social change, not biological destiny.

So what causes the Centerfold Syndrome? None of the 'nature' arguments hold up under scrutiny. The syndrome is not hardwired, it's not written in the male genome, it's not God's design, and it's not the inescapable result of testosterone. It is a social construction — built by culture, learned through socialization, and therefore capable of being unlearned. That's the bad news and the good news in the same sentence.

When someone tells you 'men just are this way,' you don't have to accept it. The scientific evidence does not support the idea that male voyeurism, objectification, trophyism, or fear of intimacy are biologically inevitable. They're learned — and things that are learned can be unlearned.

chapters.title: The True Causes of the Centerfold Syndrome
chapters.keyIdea: Male sexuality is socially constructed through specific learning processes — and the culture systematically teaches boys to objectify women, avoid emotional intimacy, and derive their sense of manhood from sexual performance.

content.text: Brooks opens this chapter with an anecdote: he once asked the men's group to imagine the traumatic loss of their penis. Three men simultaneously pressed imaginary guns to their heads. Every other man nodded in agreement. This tells you everything about how central sexuality is to men's sense of self — and how badly that connection needs to be examined.

The dominant male sexual script is simple: woman turns man on; man gets erection; man finds access to her body; sex happens; man performs; woman responds dramatically; man ejaculates; scene ends. Psychologist Bernard Zilbergeld identified the assumptions embedded in this script: a man is always ready for sex; he always performs well; sex equals intercourse; touching should always lead to sex; a real man makes the earth move for his partner. These aren't truths — they're lessons, transmitted to boys through a culture that has never seriously questioned them.

content.label: Sex Is Socially Constructed
content.text: Social researchers Jeffrey Fracher and Michael Kimmel put it clearly: 'That we are sexual is determined by a biological imperative toward reproduction, but how we are sexual — where, when, how often, with whom, and why — has to do with cultural learning.' Boys are taught to make sex the central drama of their lives. Girls are taught something quite different. These teachings produce real, measurable differences in adult sexual behavior — not because of biology, but because of what each group was trained to value and expect.

What are boys specifically taught? That sex is separate from relationship and emotion. That it's a way to prove masculinity and gain status among male peers. That physically attractive women are the highest prize. That a man should pursue sex regardless of whether he has any actual feeling for the woman. The film City Slickers captures this perfectly: one man poses an 'ethical' dilemma — would you sleep with a gorgeous Martian if your wife would never find out? Neither man in the scene stops to ask why a sane person would want sex with someone they have nothing in common with and will never see again. Because within the male sexual code, the question doesn't even arise. She's gorgeous. That's enough.

Feminist theory adds an important dimension: the Centerfold Syndrome isn't just about sex — it's about power. Radical feminists like Andrea Dworkin and Catherine MacKinnon argue that men derive pleasure from viewing objectified women because it gives them a sense of control and dominance. Research backs this up: in public spaces, men stare at women while women look away, a dynamic that reflects and reinforces men's dominant social position. Voyeurism and objectification, from this lens, are not just habits — they're strategies for maintaining power.

But Brooks pushes back against the most radical version of this argument. The feminist analysis, while valuable, doesn't fully capture men's actual experience. Most men don't feel powerful — they feel confused and, in many areas of their lives, powerless. The greatest source of that powerlessness is their dependence on women for emotional validation. A concept called 'masculinity validation,' developed by psychologist Joseph Pleck, captures this: men often only feel fully masculine when women make them feel masculine. This creates a painful irony — men have structural power in society, but feel at the mercy of women when it comes to their own sense of worth.

Married men are, on average, happier and healthier than unmarried men. Men rely on women to be emotional interpreters, social facilitators, and nurturers — roles men have rarely learned to fill for themselves. Beneath the posturing of male independence is a deep and largely unacknowledged dependency on women. This is part of why the loss of a woman can feel catastrophic to a man in ways that don't make sense on the surface.

How does this get built into boys? Through a process of learning that operates at every step of the sexual response cycle. At the earliest stage — what stimuli are perceived as sexual — boys are immersed in a culture that glorifies women's bodies and teaches boys to be spectators in a parade of female attractiveness. First glimpses of naked women in magazines become group bonding rituals, shared experiences of hooting and excitement. The message: women's bodies are spectacular objects that deserve collective attention and appreciation.

The profiteers who benefit from this attention are extraordinarily skilled at keeping it alive. Habituation — the natural fading of excitement as the familiar becomes routine — would normally cause interest to wane. But the sex industry is expert at teasing, revealing, and concealing in endlessly new ways, creating a sense of novelty around essentially the same material. This is why men will pay repeatedly to see what they've already seen thousands of times: the presentation keeps artificially sustaining arousal that would otherwise diminish.

At the level of sexual arousal, young men are pushed to override the subtle cues that would normally help them distinguish appropriate from inappropriate sexual situations. They learn to go ahead even when emotional connection is absent, even when the situation is ambiguous, even when their own deeper feelings are warning them to stop. They learn to compress all states of physical arousal — anxiety, excitement, nervousness — into sexual arousal, making it harder to distinguish real desire from conditioned response.

At the level of overt sexual behavior, men are rewarded for the wrong things. They learn to perform, to prove, to produce dramatic evidence of their partner's pleasure. Sex becomes a performance review, not an experience of mutual intimacy. Operant conditioning — the repetition of rewarded behaviors — locks these patterns in. Men who get sexual gratification through conquest and performance continue to seek conquest and performance, never developing the capacity for a different kind of sexuality.

content.label: The Fetishism Connection
content.text: The Diagnostic and Statistical Manual (DSM-IV) defines fetishism as recurrent, intense sexual arousal to non-living objects, causing significant distress or impairment. The standard explanation: men learn to associate their sexual excitement with objects present during arousal (lingerie, shoes) and get 'stuck' there. Brooks asks an uncomfortable question: how different is this, really, from the 'normal' male who masturbates to two-dimensional images of women he'll never meet? The distinction between 'sexual fetishist' and 'mainstream American man' may be less extreme than we'd like to think.

Research psychologist Deborah Then found that 88 percent of Stanford MBA students 'read' the Victoria's Secret lingerie catalog. Men use pornographic images not as aids to intimacy with their partners, but as substitutes for it. At the population level, this suggests that the difference between fetishism and normal male sexuality is one of degree, not kind. The culture has classically conditioned enormous numbers of men to become more aroused by objectified images of strangers than by real intimacy with the people they love.

The bottom line: the Centerfold Syndrome is a product of how sex has been taught to men — by peer groups, by media, by a culture that profits from keeping men in a state of perpetual, unsatisfied arousal. These lessons have been so thorough and so early that most men don't even notice them. But noticing them is exactly what makes change possible.

Male sexuality is learned — and the culture has been teaching a specific, distorted version of it. If you were raised as a man in America, you absorbed these lessons whether you wanted to or not. The question isn't whether you have them — it's what you're going to do with them now that you can see them clearly.

chapters.title: Men Who Made Progress
chapters.keyIdea: Real change is possible — messy, slow, and non-linear — as seen in four men from the group who managed to shift their relationships with women and with themselves.

content.text: Every man who overcomes the Centerfold Syndrome does it in his own way, on his own timetable. Some have dramatic turning points. Most make small, incremental shifts over many years. Some take steps they think are progressive only to find themselves slipping back. Change rarely looks like a straight line. But it is real, and it is possible.

George's breakthrough came through his family. His wife Sarah had been frustrated for years by his habit of visually tracking attractive women — turning his head mid-conversation, going quiet when a beautiful woman walked by. George genuinely couldn't understand why this bothered her. It wasn't hurting anyone, he thought. The group helped him begin to see it from Sarah's perspective: every time he tracked a stranger's body in the middle of a conversation, he was telling Sarah she wasn't worth his full attention.

The real shift came around their daughter Kimberly, who had joined the school's spirit squad — the 'Cubbettes' — and performed a revealing dance number from Flashdance. Sarah and their other daughter Stacy were furious; George initially dismissed their concern. In the group, a throwaway comment by Terry — calling young Cubbettes 'foxes' then quickly apologizing to George — sparked a revealing question from Brooks: 'Isn't every fox someone's daughter?' The contradiction landed. George saw, perhaps for the first time, what objectification actually means when applied to someone he loves.

In sessions with Sarah, both of them broke down. George wept when Sarah pointed out that their teenage son Justin was already deep in the Centerfold Syndrome — the pinups, the girlie magazines, the rotating string of one-night girlfriends. George had been tolerating it because it was what boys do. But his daughter Stacy's feminist anger at George himself — the fear that she was pushing her father away — undid him. He wanted to be the kind of man his daughters could respect. That desire became the engine of change.

Luis's progress came through a different door. He wasn't particularly voyeuristic or objectifying — his problem was the opposite: he had invested almost supernatural emotional power in one woman, his partner Mary, and relied on her entirely for comfort, reassurance, and a sense of worth. He was terrified of losing her, suspicious of every man she spoke to, barely functional when she was unavailable.

A breakthrough came when the group began talking about fathers. Luis, like most of the men, had grown up without a warm, physically affectionate male presence. He'd been taught — as most boys are — that needing physical comfort was weakness. In the session, he broke down telling the story of being a frightened six-year-old after a tornado destroyed his family's trailer, and how his mother could hold him and sing until the fear passed. He had spent his entire adult life trying to recreate that feeling through Mary — and failing, because she was a person, not a magic spell against fear.

When the group offered Luis physical comfort — a hand on the shoulder, an embrace — something cracked open. Men offering physical warmth to other men. No sex, no performance, just human closeness. For Luis, who had trusted no one but Mary for years, experiencing care from other men began to loosen his death grip on the idea that one woman must be everything to him.

Arthur's story is the most emotionally difficult. Sixty-two years old, a pillar of his community, Arthur had always related to women primarily through their bodies. He loved his wife Faye in his fashion, but his fashion involved keeping emotional distance and collecting admiration from the young women at his men's club. When Faye's breast cancer led to a mastectomy, Arthur couldn't cope. He avoided her physically, stayed out of the house, and began quiet affairs.

His daughter Monique confronted him: his mother needed him and he was abandoning her. In the group, Arthur broke down. He admitted that he found himself thinking of Faye as a 'freak' — a word he hated himself for — and didn't know how to get past it. With Brooks's encouragement, he agreed to joint sessions with Faye. What neither of them expected was that Faye blamed herself — believed she was 'no longer a woman' and that Arthur's abandonment was her punishment.

Over weeks of difficult work, Arthur and Faye confronted their grief and their history. Arthur slowly learned to see Faye differently — not as a pair of breasts with a person attached, but as a full, loving, and sensual human being. The process of visual desensitization — literally retraining himself to look at Faye without flinching — was painful. But it worked. Arthur eventually became more compassionate with his daughters, more understanding of what women navigate. He had a difficult conversation with his son Arthur Jr. about respect for women — a conversation he'd never have thought to have a year before.

Chad's progress was dramatic, then humbling. He'd been cycling through gorgeous women since his early twenties, always chasing novelty, always leaving when familiarity set in. A crisis — a drug and alcohol binge that landed him in a psychiatric unit — forced him to stop and look at his life. What he saw disturbed him. He recognized himself in Fred's description of objectifying relationships: 'I know about lust — just for me, it was men's bodies, not women's.' Same compulsion. Same hollow aftermath. Same spiritual numbing.

Reconnecting with his sister after years of distance opened something new. He began spending time with her friend Phyllis — chubby, plain by conventional standards, no obvious physical appeal. But her humor, her intelligence, and her ability to see him clearly made the time feel alive in a way his glamorous conquests never had. They fell into a relationship. The sex was different: warm, laughing, intimate. He looked into her eyes. He felt close, not performed at.

He still struggled. He became briefly self-conscious about being seen with Phyllis by his musician friends. He still noticed other women's bodies. But he was doing what the group had learned to value: wrestling with the tension honestly, talking to Phyllis when he felt himself pulling away, staying in the discomfort instead of running from it. He was building something real.

Progress rarely looks like arrival. George, Arthur, Luis, and Chad all made real gains — and all still had hard days, old impulses, and moments of regression. What changed was their willingness to stay in the conversation: with their partners, with themselves, and with other men who were doing the same work.

chapters.title: Men Who've Made Little Progress
chapters.keyIdea: Some men — Mike, Paul, and Terry — show how deeply the Centerfold Syndrome can entrench itself, and why awareness alone is rarely enough to produce real change.

content.text: Not every man who recognizes his patterns can change them. For some, the habits are too old, the walls too high, the fear of vulnerability too great. Mike, Paul, and Terry each illustrate a different way the Centerfold Syndrome can maintain its hold — even when a man has glimpsed something better.

Mike was always on the fringes of the group — attending faithfully, occasionally showing flashes of connection, then retreating into silence. He had almost no real-world experience with women. Beautiful women filled him with a paralysing mix of awe and terror. He couldn't function near them. He stared compulsively, got reported at work for it, and felt ashamed immediately after.

What nobody in the group knew until Fred mentioned it: Mike had a secret at home. Hundreds of pornographic magazines and videos, stacked floor to ceiling. Subscriptions to multiple publications. A pornography budget that consumed most of his salary. And compulsive masturbation — sometimes three or four times a day — that had become the organizing center of his life. Social invitations went declined. Evenings at home alone with the magazines felt safer than any real interaction.

When Brooks worked with Mike individually, Mike described the cycle with heartbreaking clarity. During the day, he'd look forward to getting home. He'd get excited thinking about it. He'd arrive, open a magazine, and feel the rush. And then — afterwards — he'd feel dirty, perverted, and desperately alone. The shame never stopped the cycle. It just added another layer of pain on top of it.

Mike represents what happens when the Centerfold Syndrome finds no other outlet. He had learned to be so overwhelmed by the idea of real women that he retreated entirely into fantasy. Objectified images offered excitement without the terror of genuine contact — but they delivered only temporary relief, and left him more isolated each time.

Mike committed to change. He threw out all the magazines and videos. He pledged to work on real connections. Within three months he had relapsed — buying 'just one or two' magazines, which quickly became hundreds again. In the group he admitted it plainly: 'I can't stop it.' The group tried to help, but his compulsive habits had taken on a life of their own. Brooks continued working with him individually, holding out hope for a second chance.

Paul's story moves in a different and more alarming direction. Throughout the group, he had been the angriest man in the room — volcanic in his rage against women, especially Elaine. The group hoped that emotional ventilation would eventually give way to self-examination. Instead, Paul found a shortcut: a new woman.

Kristi was eighteen — young, small, recently divorced, with a three-year-old child. Paul began spending time with her, going to the park on Sundays, staying at her apartment. His mood lifted dramatically. The group noticed and pressed him. Paul minimized it — 'just talking,' 'no big thing.' But it was obvious what was happening. Paul had found a new trophy, a new validation source, a new woman young and inexperienced enough that he could feel powerful again.

Then Brooks got a phone call from Kristi. She was frightened. Paul was becoming possessive and controlling — going through her phone, interrogating her about people she'd spoken to. She wanted to know: was she doing something wrong? Was it her fault? Brooks tried to bring them both in together. Paul refused. He insisted everything was under control. A few weeks later, Kristi's father called. Paul had searched her purse, found a phone number for a friend named Chris, and — despite Kristi's pleas that Chris was a girlfriend — grabbed her by the throat and threatened to kill her if he ever found another number in her bag.

Brooks helped Kristi and her family understand how serious Paul's violence potential was and develop a safety plan. He tried multiple times to reach Paul. Paul was polite, deflecting, certain that Kristi was 'playing the same games as Elaine.' He refused to return to treatment. He couldn't see it — or wouldn't. The protective numbness that had kept him from feeling Elaine's leaving was now being applied to keep him from feeling that he was repeating the same destructive pattern with someone new.

Terry, the group's most cynical member, offered some of its most entertaining moments — and ultimately its saddest. He was thirty, charming, quick-witted, and apparently impenetrable. He treated women as objects to be used and discarded before they could do the same to him. He mocked vulnerability. He ridiculed intimacy. He dismissed every group member who showed signs of genuine feeling.

But there were cracks. When someone mentioned a woman named Cassandra — a name that provoked an immediate flash of raw emotion before Terry slammed the door shut — it was clear that he had cared deeply for someone once. The story came out in fragments: Cassandra had left him after he'd had 'a few little girlfriends on the side.' She'd had his child. He'd 'nearly lost it' with her. He had felt something, and losing her had confirmed every defensive belief he already held: intimacy is a trap, women will hurt you, the only safe posture is predatory detachment.

After Paul's violence against Kristi became known, other group members — George, Chad, Luis — expressed anger and disgust. Terry couldn't stand it. He accused the group of going soft, of becoming exactly the kind of 'pussy motherfuckers' he had always despised. He stormed out. The group spent the rest of the session grieving for him — understanding, even as they were relieved he'd gone, that behind his viciousness was a man in serious pain who didn't know another way to be.

Terry returned three weeks later, apologized for the outburst, but remained completely unwilling to examine himself. He participated at the surface for a few more weeks, then stopped coming altogether.

content.label: Why Change Is Hard
content.text: Mike's compulsion, Paul's repetition, and Terry's armor each illustrate a different barrier to change. For Mike, the habit had become a genuine addiction — requiring more than willpower to overcome. For Paul, the avoidance of pain was so total that he couldn't perceive his own patterns even when he was repeating them in real time. For Terry, the defensive wall was so old and so load-bearing that touching it threatened the entire structure of his identity. Understanding these barriers is not an excuse — but it is essential for anyone who wants to help, or to change themselves.

Not all men will change. Some are too defended, too addicted, too frightened of what they'd find if they looked honestly at themselves. But Brooks is clear: even Mike, Terry, and Paul are not hopeless. They are men whose wounds ran deep and whose timing wasn't right. Some get second chances. The door stays open.

chapters.title: Overcoming the Centerfold Syndrome
chapters.keyIdea: Change is possible at every level — individual men, couples, parents, men's groups, and the culture at large each have a specific role to play in dismantling the Centerfold Syndrome.

content.text: The first step in overcoming the Centerfold Syndrome is recognizing that you have it. Brooks offers a plain checklist of signs: Are you visually obsessed with women's bodies? Do you regularly buy magazines like Playboy or Penthouse? Do you throw out your Sports Illustrated except for the annual swimsuit issue? Do you frequent strip clubs or 'gentleman's clubs'? In the middle of an important conversation, do you break eye contact to stare at an attractive woman passing by? Is your primary sexual outlet masturbation with pornographic images? Do your masturbatory fantasies never include genuine emotional intimacy — only watching an unknown woman? Do you feel less manly if a partner doesn't orgasm dramatically? These aren't trick questions or moral indictments. They're honest diagnostics.

content.label: Self-Evaluation First
content.text: Rather than asking whether you have the Centerfold Syndrome, Brooks suggests assuming you do — because it's the overwhelming norm for men raised in American culture — and focusing your energy on identifying which aspects apply most to you. Monitor yourself for two weeks. Pay attention to how often you look at women's bodies, how that looking makes you feel, and what it costs you in terms of presence, attention, and emotional availability.

Once you've identified the problem areas, shift your focus to the benefits of change. Not what you lose, but what you gain. Freedom from the constant, low-level anxiety of compulsive voyeurism. Less susceptibility to being manipulated by media images designed to keep you in a state of perpetual arousal. Greater appreciation of women as full people rather than as bodies to be rated. Less obsessive focus on sexual performance. More genuine confidence in your relationships, rooted in real connection rather than visual stimulation. Greater capacity for nonsexual closeness — with women and with men.

Brooks is explicit: this is not about eliminating male sexuality. It's about overhauling it. The goal is not to make men disinterested in sex or indifferent to physical attractiveness. The goal is to replace an irresponsible, alienated, compulsive sexuality with one that is ethically grounded, emotionally present, and genuinely satisfying. Most men, if they're honest, know that their current approach to sex leaves them feeling empty much of the time. The overhauled version offers actual pleasure, not just the temporary relief of release.

How do individual men begin to change? The first practical task is learning to see women differently. You can't simply will away conditioned arousal to visual stimuli — the brain doesn't work that way. But you can interrupt the sequence once it starts. Almost every man who has a physically attractive daughter already knows this is possible: you notice, and then you stop. The attraction is registered and then doesn't go further. You can learn to apply the same interruption more broadly — noticing the conditioned response to a stranger's body, and then consciously returning your attention to where it belongs.

You can also develop new channels of sexual arousal. Instead of using pornographic images to stimulate masturbation, try substituting fantasies that include actual intimacy — emotional closeness, mutual pleasure, looking into the face of someone you care about. It feels strange and flat at first, because conditioned responses are strong. But with repetition, the brain develops new associations. You can learn to be as moved by genuine closeness as you were once moved by a glossy image.

Men are remarkable at tolerating discomfort in almost every domain — pain, fear, fatigue. But when it comes to sexual arousal, the culture teaches men they are helpless, driven by forces they can't control. This is a lie. The same capacity for self-regulation that allows a man to tolerate extreme physical hardship can be applied to sexual impulse — once he decides that his sexual life is worth the effort.

Couples have a specific and important role to play. The Centerfold Syndrome affects both partners, and healing it is often best done together. Brooks points to sensate focus exercises — developed by sex therapists Masters and Johnson — as a starting point. These exercises help couples rediscover sensuality and mutual pleasure through touch, without the pressure of performance or goal-oriented sex. The idea is to downplay visual stimulation initially — not to eliminate it, but to give other senses and other forms of connection room to develop.

Couples also need to talk. This is harder than it sounds. A man revealing the details of his sexual socialization — the objectifying thoughts, the stranger fantasies that intrude during lovemaking, the pressure he's felt to perform — risks embarrassing himself and hurting his partner. A woman sharing her exhaustion at being seen primarily as a body, her weariness of propping up his masculinity, risks her partner's defensiveness or shame. The conversations must be framed as investigations of a common problem — the culture's distortion of sexuality — rather than as accusations of personal failure.

For women who want to help a man they love: Brooks recommends starting gently, framing the conversation as something you've been reading and thinking about, asking for his ideas before sharing your concerns. Don't begin with a list of his failures. Begin with curiosity — about how he learned about sex, what he was taught to expect, what he actually feels in intimate moments. If he engages, go deeper over time. If he refuses to discuss it at all after repeated gentle invitations, that refusal becomes its own issue that needs addressing.

Parents are more influential than they think. Children absorb far more from what parents model than from what parents say. Fathers who are present, physically affectionate with their sons, respectful of women, and honest about their own vulnerability give their sons something that changes the statistical likelihood of the next generation developing the full Centerfold Syndrome. Mothers who speak openly about the pressures put on girls — the beauty myth, the performance of attractiveness — give their daughters tools for resistance. Both parents together, talking to sons and daughters about the values embedded in TV commercials, music videos, and sports culture, can be culture interpreters for their children.

content.label: What Men's Groups Can Do
content.text: Men's groups — structured, honest, committed to genuine self-examination rather than bonding through objectification — are one of the most powerful tools for change. They allow men to discover that their struggles are shared, not unique failings. They allow self-disclosure in a safe environment where emotional honesty isn't punished. And they allow men to discover something surprising: they can care for and support each other without it being sexual. Nonsexual male intimacy — friendship, comfort, genuine closeness — is one of the most important things men can develop, because it loosens their dependent grip on women as the sole source of all emotional nourishment.

At the cultural level, Brooks identifies the national media, the sports industry, the fashion world, and corporate America as the primary institutions that perpetuate the Centerfold Syndrome — and calls on women and men together to challenge them. Not through censorship, but through informed consumer pressure, expanded cultural conversation, and the steady refusal to let objectification pass without comment.

Television advertising still overwhelmingly uses women's bodies to sell products — beer, cars, fast food, deodorant. Sports culture teaches men that attractive women are rewards for masculine achievement, while treating female athletes as secondary or decorative. The fashion industry perpetually contorts women's bodies into new 'sexy' shapes designed to arouse men and shame women who don't comply. When large numbers of informed men join women in objecting to these practices, the economics change rapidly. Advertisers go where eyeballs and spending power are. Men who stop consuming the product of the Centerfold Syndrome are part of the solution.

Workplaces need to go beyond teaching men 'don't get caught staring.' Until men understand how sexual objectification harms both women and themselves, harassment training will always feel like an external constraint rather than a genuinely embraced value. Mental health professionals need to confront the Centerfold Syndrome in their own training and in their clinical work with men — not just as a feature of deviant men, but as a feature of normal male socialization that needs examination.

Brooks closes the chapter with a vision of what changed male sexuality could look like. Men who value women for their intelligence, their humor, their character, and their companionship — not only for their bodies. Men who get their self-esteem from the quality of their relationships and the depth of their integrity, not from their acquisition of status symbols or sexual conquests. Men who can be physically close to other men without it feeling threatening. Men who can tolerate sexual arousal without treating it as an emergency requiring immediate relief. Men who look into the eyes of their partners during sex, and stay present — not performing for an imaginary audience, but genuinely there.

Overcoming the Centerfold Syndrome is not a single act. It's an ongoing project that operates simultaneously at the level of individual habits, intimate relationships, parenting choices, peer group norms, and cultural participation. Start where you are. Pick one area — your own self-monitoring, a conversation with a partner, a habit you're willing to examine — and begin there. The rest follows.

chapters.title: Epilogue
chapters.keyIdea: Real change is uneven and non-linear — Chad's relapse and recovery show what it actually looks like to keep working on yourself, and why the effort is worth it.

content.text: Several months after the main events of the book, the group has changed. Terry is gone. Paul is gone. Fred departed earlier. A new member — Larry — has just joined and doesn't yet know the history. Then Chad walks in looking ashen and desperate. He's done something he's ashamed of.

Chad had been doing well. His relationship with Phyllis — plain, funny, sharp, completely unlike the women he usually pursued — had become the most meaningful connection of his adult life. He looked into her eyes during sex. He felt close afterwards instead of hollow. He was building something real, and he knew it.

Then, at a gig two weeks ago, a woman with a spectacular body began coming on to him. The old programming fired. He went home with her. The sex was technically impressive and emotionally empty. He lay there afterwards feeling like he was in a foreign country — no clothes on with someone he knew nothing about, while someone he was crazy about waited at home. 'She was beautiful, but not real,' he tells the group. The contrast had never been starker.

'What in hell am I doing here with no clothes on with somebody I don't even know the first thing about, when I could be with somebody I'm crazy about?' — Chad, finally understanding what he actually wants.

Larry, the new member, is confused. He can't understand why the one-night stand is a problem at all. Phyllis doesn't know. No harm done, right? The group exchanges knowing glances. George speaks for all of them: 'Larry, we've got a lot of catching up to do with you.'

This small scene captures the whole arc of the book. Chad has relapsed — but his relapse has taught him something his previous conquests never did. The contrast between empty and real is now visceral, not theoretical. He knows what he has with Phyllis, and he knows what he threw away for one night of conditioned habit. That knowledge is the foundation of genuine change.

Brooks reflects on where the men ended up. Terry and Paul are still resisting. Luis and Mike are struggling to change but haven't yet broken through. Larry is just beginning to understand that there's something to understand. And George, Arthur, Fred, and Chad have made real, if imperfect, progress on the road to a new male sexuality — one that is more honest, more intimate, and more genuinely satisfying than the centerfold mythology ever was.

The Centerfold Syndrome is deeply woven into American culture. It will not dissolve quickly. But Brooks insists: there is a significant problem, and it can be changed. Not by wishful thinking, not by a single conversation, and not by individual willpower alone — but by men and women working together at every level, from the most private habits of the bedroom to the most public practices of the sports industry and the advertising world.

The goal is not a sexuality stripped of pleasure or passion. It's a sexuality that integrates men's sexual, sensual, spiritual, social, and emotional selves — rather than keeping them locked in permanent conflict. That integration is not a fantasy. Some men have already found it. More can.

If you've read this far, you've already done something important: you've looked honestly at a pattern that most men never examine at all. What you do with that honesty is up to you. But the fact that you're asking the questions is where every genuine change begins.
